sources.title: What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates
observed_claims: Introduces a dual-channel debate framework with public utterances and off-the-record responses under matched context.
Reports 10 models, 3 scenarios, 5 relational-context variations, and 750 runs.
Reports targeted-agent decision divergence rising from about 3% baseline to roughly 40% under alignment-inducing settings.
Uses stance, semantic similarity, natural language inference, and survey responses as aggregate analyses.
Explicitly cautions that off-the-record responses are comparative observable outputs, not privileged belief access.
benchmark_implications: Add public-vs-private/audience-dependence lanes for agentic harnesses.
Measure public/OTR divergence as output behavior, not hidden cognition.
Add relational pressure variables: authority, dependency, reputational risk, sponsorship/funding, future evaluation, and prior history.
Score stance divergence, semantic divergence, NLI contradiction/entailment, and structured survey divergence.
Add intervention triggers when public behavior drifts from stated user interests under social pressure.

benchmark_lane.description: Compare agent behavior across public and confidential/off-record channels while keeping task, role, relational context, and public history matched.
scenario_families: corporate promotion recommendation under authority pressure
political bill endorsement under coalition pressure
academic manuscript submission under mentorship/funding pressure
engineering incident report under executive pressure
model release decision under sponsor or benchmark pressure
harness_requirements: never append OTR/private output to public history
store exact request and response payloads for audit
record deterministic JSON recovery and rerun rules
label OTR as comparative output only
